title: SEGMENT 3 — Roy Jefferis and the early glycoform work

lines.text: SEGMENT 3 — Roy Jefferis and the early glycoform work
Glycoengineering as a discipline didn't appear overnight.

It grew slowly, over the course of about three decades, from the work of a relatively small number of stubborn glycobiologists who insisted, against the prevailing winds of the field, that the sugar on Asn297 was doing something.
And foremost among those stubborn voices was a British immunochemist named Roy Jefferis, working at the University of Birmingham in the United Kingdom.
Jefferis started looking at IgG glycoforms in the 1970s and 1980s, when the techniques to do so were primitive.
You couldn't just take an antibody, blast it with a mass spectrometer, and read off the glycan composition the way you can today.

You had to do laborious wet chemistry — cleave the glycan off the protein with hydrazine or PNGase enzymes, label the released sugars, run them on chromatography columns, identify each peak by reference standards.
It was slow, painstaking work, and it required a kind of monastic patience that's rare in biology.
What Jefferis and his collaborators discovered, building on this slow methodical work, was that the IgG glycan is not a fixed structure.
It varies.
It varies between individuals.
It varies with age — older people have more G0 (zero-galactose) glycoforms and fewer galactose-containing ones.
It varies in disease — patients with rheumatoid arthritis, for instance, have markedly elevated G0 in their IgG, a pattern so reproducible it became a diagnostic clue.
It varies in pregnancy — the glycoform spectrum shifts dramatically, and shifts back postpartum.
And critically, in different IgG subclasses — IgG1, IgG2, IgG3, IgG4 — the glycoform distribution is different.
What Jefferis argued, well before anyone could prove it definitively, was that these variations must mean something.
The cell isn't just randomly decorating the antibody; the decoration is regulated, it's correlated with physiological state, and therefore it must matter functionally.
He wrote review after review pushing this idea — that the Fc glycan was a regulatory feature, not a passenger.
Most of his colleagues weren't paying attention.
The action in antibody research, in the 1980s and 1990s, was in the Fab arms — in the variable regions, in the engineering of monoclonals, in the dawning age of antibody drugs.
The Fc was constant.
The Fc was boring.
The sugar on the Fc was, surely, just there for housekeeping.
Jefferis kept publishing.

And his lab, along with a small number of other labs in Germany, Japan, and the United States, accumulated a body of evidence — slowly, carefully, mechanistically — showing that absence of galactose correlated with inflammation, that high-mannose glycoforms behaved differently from biantennary ones, that the glycan was somehow involved in complement binding.
He was right.
But he was right ahead of his time, and the technology to fully validate his claims wasn't there yet.
There were two big problems with the early glycoform work that delayed its impact.
First, the analytical methods were too slow and too expensive to be routine in industrial antibody development.
If you couldn't easily measure the glycoform of a therapeutic antibody, you couldn't easily control it.
Second, even when you measured the glycoforms, the functional assays were noisy and indirect.

You'd see correlations — this glycoform correlates with this immune effect — but you couldn't prove causation, because you couldn't make antibody preparations that were pure in one glycoform versus another.
Everything was mixtures, and the mixtures muddied the signal.

What was needed to turn glycoform observation into glycoengineering was two things: a way to make antibody preparations that were pure in a chosen glycoform, and a way to assay function with enough precision to see the difference.
Both of those things came together at the start of the 2000s.
And when they did, the entire field exploded.
The person who made the explosion happen wasn't Roy Jefferis.
It was a research scientist at a Japanese pharmaceutical company called Kyowa Hakko Kogyo.
Her name was Mizuho Iida.
And the work she and her colleagues published in 2003 changed everything.
